# A high strength Al–Li alloy produced by laser powder bed fusion: Densification, microstructure, and mechanical properties

Yang Qi $ ^{a} $, Hu Zhang $ ^{a,b,*} $, Xiaojia Nie $ ^{a} $, Zhiheng Hu $ ^{a} $, Haihong Zhu $ ^{a} $, Xiaoyan Zeng $ ^{a} $

 $ ^{a} $ Wuhan National Laboratory for Optoelectronics, Huazhong University of Science and Technology, Wuhan, Hubei 430074, PR China

 $ ^{b} $ School of Optical and Electronic Information, Huazhong University of Science and Technology, Wuhan, Hubei 430074, PR China

##### ARTICLE INFO

Keywords:

Laser powder bed fusion

Al–Li alloys

Microstructure

Mechanical properties

High strength

### A B S T R A C T

High strength 2195 Al–Li alloy samples produced by laser powder bed fusion (LPBF) were obtained in this study. The microstructure and mechanical properties were investigated for both the as-fabricated and T6 heat-treated samples. The as-fabricated sample showed a coarse columnar grain microstructure, heterogeneous element distribution, high density of dislocations, and complex precipitation types. After T6 heat treatment, the dominate strengthening T $ _{1} $ (Al $ _{2} $CuLi) plates precipitated in the matrix, and the dislocation density remained high. The as-fabricated 2195 sample showed a yield strength (YS) of 207.7 ± 1.5 MPa, an ultimate tensile strength (UTS) of 340.7 ± 2.9 MPa, and elongation of 17.2 ± 0.9%. After T6 heat treatment, the YS and UTS were dramatically increased to 521.3 ± 14.4 MPa and 587.0 ± 7.2 MPa, and the elongation was 6.9 ± 0.3%. The LPBF of the high strength Al–Li alloy was successfully confirmed.

### 1. Introduction

Laser powder bed fusion (LPBF) of aluminum alloys is receiving great attention from researchers and industrialists [1–3]. Most printable aluminum alloys are Al-Si based casting aluminum alloys, such as Al-Si10Mg [4,5], Al-12Si [6,7], and Al-7Si-0.6 Mg [8]. The as-fabricated samples of these alloys feature moderate strength and ductility [9,10]. To further improve the mechanical properties, many researchers have studied the heat treatment effects on AlSi10Mg produced by LPBF [11]. Although the ductility can be significantly improved [12], the highest ultimate tensile strength (UTS) is smaller than 500 MPa [13,14]. Decorating AlSi10Mg with nano-TiB $ _{2} $ particles can improve the UTS of AlSi10Mg produced by LPBF to 530 MPa [15]. However, it only reaches the typical performance of the 2xxx and 7xxx high strength wrought aluminum alloys.

For these high strength wrought aluminum alloys, hot cracks are difficult to prevent in the intolerable microstructures during the LPBF process. By optimizing the processing parameters, the UTS of the nearly full dense and crack-free Al–Cu–Mg parts is 402 MPa [16], and can be improved to 532 MPa after heat treatment [17]. The 7xxx series aluminum alloys are difficult to fabricate by LPBF in a wide range of processing parameters without cracks [18,19]. Adding grain refiners has been demonstrated extensively as an effective method of producing crack-free high strength aluminum alloy [20–26]. These grain refiners can promote heterogeneous nucleation sites and induce columnar to equiaxed transition to reduce crack susceptibility. However, the strength of most modified high strength aluminum alloys is lower than 500 MPa [20,26–28]. One exception is an Al-4.5Mn-1.3Mg-0.8Sc-0.7 Zr alloy. This alloy was developed with good LPBF processability [29], and a UTS of 570 MPa was achieved [30]. But the alloying elements of Mn, Sc, and Zr increase the density of aluminum alloy. For the eternal pursuit of high performance and lightweight, there is still a strong motivation to seek stronger and lighter aluminum alloys for LPBF.

Al–Li alloys offer higher performance and lighter weight than conventional high strength aluminum alloy in traditional manufacturing [31,32]. However, there are few reports on the LPBF of Al–Li alloys. Al–Li alloys are susceptible to hot cracks during laser processing due to their large solidification range, high thermal expansion coefficient, and the low melting point eutectic formation during solidification [33]. Recently, Raffias [34] produced a crack-free dense AA2099 Al–Cu–Li alloy LPBF part, but the highest microhardness was only 72.8 HV and no tensile properties were reported.

In this study, we chose the 2195 alloy as a demonstration Al–Li alloy for LPBF. The 2195 Al–Cu–Li–Mg–Ag–Zr alloy is one of the representative latest generations of Al–Li alloys and has wide application prospects in the military and aerospace industries [35–38]. We studied the feasibility of the 2195 alloy produced by LPBF. The processing parameters were optimized to obtain crack-free and nearly full dense

samples. The microstructure and the mechanical properties were investigated in the as-fabricated samples and heat-treated samples. Results demonstrated that high strength Al–Li alloy can be manufactured by LPBF.

### 2. Materials and methods

#### 2.1. Materials

Gas-atomized Al–Cu–Li–Mg–Ag–Zr powders supplied by Intelligence Manufacturing Technology were the starting material in this study. Fig. 1 displays the powder morphology with the particle size distribution. The raw powder particles exhibit nearly spherical shape and have a mean particle size of 35.3  $ \mu $m. The inductively coupled plasma-optical emission spectrometry (ICP-OES) was used to analyze the chemical composition of the powder. Table 1 lists the chemical composition of the powder and the standard chemical composition range of AA2195.

#### 2.2. Sample preparations

The additive manufacturing of the powders was performed on a self-developed LPBF machine at room temperature under a protective argon atmosphere. The specifications of the LPBF machine were given elsewhere [39]. A series of experiments by building 8 mm (length) × 8 mm (width) × 4 mm (height) cubic samples were performed for optimizing the processing parameters. The processing parameters used for the optimization were: the laser power of 200 W, the hatch spacing of 0.1 mm, the powder layer thickness of 40 μm and 30 μm, the laser scanning velocity of 80 – 320 mm/s, and a hatch style rotation of 90° between consecutive layers. The chemical composition of the sample fabricated with optimized parameters was also measured by ICP-OES and was listed in Table 1. Tensile plates (30 mm (length) × 8 mm (width) × 2 mm (thickness)) were built with optimized parameters and perpendicular to the building direction. For the tensile tests, dog-bone-shaped samples with a gauge section of 12 mm (length) × 2.5 mm (width) × 1.5 mm (thickness) were machined from these tensile plates. The heat-treated samples underwent a T6 condition. They were solutionized at 520 °C for 1 h, immediately quenched with water, and subsequently

#### 2.3. Characterizations

All the samples for characterizations were prepared with the standard metallographic procedures. The optical microscope (OM) graphs were acquired by a Nikon EPIPHOT 300 optical microscope. Eight vertical section OM graphs were processed by ImagePro software to obtain the relative densities of the cubic samples. The microstructure features and the fracture surfaces were characterized by a FEI Nova Nano SEM 450 scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and a FEI Tecnai G2 F30 transmission electron microscope (TEM) equipped with an energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS). The electron backscatter diffraction (EBSD) results were collected by a Zeiss Gemini SEM 300 using a step size of 3  $ \mu $m, and the corresponding data were processed by AZTEC software. The average grain sizes were obtained based on the large angle ( $ > 15^{\circ} $) grain boundaries. The elements distribution was conducted by an electron probe microanalyzer (EPMA, EPMA-8050G). All the microstructure analyses were performed on the vertical section of the samples. The tensile tests were performed on a Shimadzu AG-100 kN tester at room temperature with an extension rate of 1 mm/min. The tensile tests were repeated three times for each type of sample. The solidification curves and the thermal-physical properties were calculated by the thermodynamic calculation software JMatPro using the Scheil-Gulliver model.

### 3. Results and discussions

#### 3.1. Densification and defects

Fig. 2 shows the optical microscope graphs of the LPBF 2195 samples produced with different scanning velocity and layer thickness. The laser power and the hatch spacing were kept 200 W and 0.1 mm. The corresponding relative densities are given in Fig. 3. Two main defects, unfused pores and cracks, are found in the OM graphs. For the samples manufactured with a fixed layer thickness of 40  $ \mu $m shown in Fig. 2(a)–(d), the relative density decreases rapidly with the increase of the scanning velocity. The decrease of the relative density is related to the increase of the unfused pores. These unfused pores are caused by the poor bonding among the scanning tracks and building layers because of the insufficient laser energy input. As shown in Fig. 2(e)–(h) and Fig. 3, the porosity of the samples fabricated with the layer thickness of 30  $ \mu $m is significantly reduced. The positive correlation between the relative density and the scanning velocity is consistent with the samples built with the layer thickness of 40  $ \mu $m. When the scanning velocity is larger than or equal to 160 mm/s, the cracks are observed for the LPBF 2195 samples regardless of the layer thickness.

### 3.2. Crack formation and inhibition mechanism

To investigate the crack initiation and propagation, EBSD characterizations were performed for the LPBF 2195 samples with and

Fig. 8. Relationship between the scanning velocity and the molten pool lifetime on the centerline of the molten pool top.

without cracks (Fig. 4). Regardless of cracks, the microstructures of all the samples mainly consist of large columnar grains. The cracks initiate at and grow along the columnar grain boundaries. And some cracks originate from unfused defects due to the notch effect. During the solidification, the columnar grains are formed along the thermal gradient direction, and the interdendritic liquid remains among the columnar grains boundaries. At the final stage of the solidification, due to lack of the liquid feeding, the solidification and thermal shrinkage lead the intergranular areas to crack. The EBSD results present that the average grain sizes of the LPBF 2195 samples fabricated with the scanning velocities of 80 mm/s, 160 mm/s, and 240 mm/s are 37.9  $ \mu $m, 33.2  $ \mu $m, and 29.8  $ \mu $m, respectively. A higher scanning velocity leads to smaller average grain size for the LPBF 2195 samples. The hot crack mechanism of the LPBF high-entropy alloy is that the formation of large grains causes severe residual stress [40]. However, the hot crack sensitivity of the LPBF 2195 samples is higher for the smaller grains.

The hot crack sensitivity of alloys can be determined by their solidification curves. The solidification curves of the 2195 and the AlSi10Mg alloys are drawn in Fig. 5. The shape of these curves is determined by the alloy composition. The solidification range of 2195 (144 °C) is much larger than that of AlSi10Mg (36 °C). It is worth noting that the solidification curve of 2195 shows a sharp decline at the high solid fraction due to the increase in solute content in the liquid phase. But the solidification curve of AlSi10Mg decreases rapidly at the low solid fraction.

Based on the solidification curves, we can draw a schematic diagram of the liquid feeding between two columnar grains for 2195 and AlSi10Mg, as shown in Fig. 6. The solidification channel of 2195 is significantly longer than that of AlSi10Mg. This longer solidification channel needs more liquid phases to prevent cracking.

The liquid phase amount is positively correlated to the molten pool (MP) lifetime. The MP lifetime on the centerline of the MP top is equal to the MP length divided by the scanning velocity. Fig. 7 displays the schematic diagram of the MP length. The MP length is the distance between the two points of the solidus isotherm on the x-axis.

The Rosenthal's equation [41] is used to calculate the MP length:

 $$ T=T_{0}+\frac{\eta P}{2\pi kR}\exp\left[\frac{-\nu(x+R)}{2\alpha}\right] $$ 

where T is the local temperature,  $ T_{0} $ is the platform temperature,  $ \eta $ is the laser absorption, P is the laser power, k is the thermal conductivity,  $ R = \sqrt{x^{2} + y^{2} + z^{2}} $ is the distance from the center of the laser beam,  $ \nu $ is the scanning velocity, and  $ \alpha $ is the thermal diffusivity. The thermal diffusivity value  $ \alpha $ can be calculated by Eq. (2):

Fig. 8 presents the calculation results of the variation of the MP lifetime with the scanning velocity. The lower MP lifetime owing to the higher scanning velocity causes the cracks to fail healing. Thus, the low scanning velocity can provide enough healing duration to inhibit the cracks for the LPBF 2195 samples.

of the as-fabricated and T6. Fig. 9 shows the SEM images of the as-fabricated LPBF 2195 sample. MP boundaries and columnar grains boundaries are clearly distinguishable from the MP interiors. The MP irregularity shown in Fig. 9(a) may be responsible for the remaining unfused defects. Combining with the EBSD result presented in Fig. 5(a), the SEM image also proves the coarse columnar grains in the microstructure. These coarse columnar grains are formed by epitaxial growth through multiple building layers. Unlike other Al-Cu alloy samples fabricated by LPBF [16,43,44], the columnar grains of the LPBF 2195 sample are wider and longer. The low thermal conductivity of 2195 leads to the low solidification rate, resulting in the wider and longer columnarity. Moreover, the content of the grain refinement element Zr in the 2195 alloy is smaller than 0.16%. The low Zr content cannot provide enough Al₃Zr nuclei for the heterogeneous nucleation sites of Al [45]. As such, lacking nucleation sites also causes the formation of large columnar grains.

There are three different forms of the precipitates (white phases) in the SEM image with higher magnification in Fig. 9(b). Some

At the final stage of the columnar grain solidification, the eutectic phases with low melting point precipitate along the columnar grain boundaries. The precipitates inside the MP are nearly granular and evenly distributed. The fast cooling rate makes these granular precipitates uniformly distributed inside the individual MP. This heterogeneous microstructure of the as-fabricated sample is further verified in the EPMA image shown in Fig. 10(a). The Al, Cu, Mg, and Ag elements are inhomogeneous in the sample. Specifically, the counts of the Cu and Ag elements at the columnar grain and MP boundaries are higher than other locations, and the Al and Mg elements display opposite characteristics. No significant Zr segregation is observed. After T6 treatment, the T6 LPBF 2195 sample displays a uniform microstructure. The Mg and Ag elements are homogeneous, and some Cu-containing particles are evenly distributed. The T6 treatment makes these alloying elements dissolved into the Al matrix and uniformly precipitate.

Fig. 11 displays the EBSD inverse pole figure maps of the as-fabricated and T6 LPBF 2195 samples. The T6 LPBF 2195 sample also performs the coarse columnarity features. Fig. 12(a)(b) presents the grain size distribution detected by the EBSD results. The average grain sizes of the as-fabricated and T6 LPBF 2195 samples are 37.9  $ \mu $m and 34.3  $ \mu $m, respectively. These similar average grain sizes indicate that the T6 treatment did not coarsen the grains of the LPBF 2195 samples. The misorientation angle distribution presented in Fig. 12(c)(d) shows that the frequencies of the misorientation angle in the range of 0~5° of the as-fabricated and T6 samples are 13.0% and 11.2%, respectively. These high frequencies indicate that both the samples perform the preference of the low angle grain boundaries (LAGBs). These LAGBs consist of a high density of dislocations to accommodate large residual thermal strains due to the rapid cooling of LPBF.

Based on the LAGBs (0~5°), the EBSD kernel average misorientation (KAM) maps of the as-fabricated and T6 LPBF 2195 samples are shown in Fig. 13. The average misorientation angles around the measured points are given by KAM and can be used to calculate the Geometrically Necessary Dislocations (GNDs) density  $ \rho_{GND} $ [47]:

 $$ \rho_{GND}=\frac{2\theta}{ub} $$ 

where  $ \theta $ is the average misorientation angle, u is the unit length (equivalent to the step size), and b is the Burgers vector (0.286 nm for aluminum [44]).  $ u = 3\mu m $ for our EBSD measurements. The calculated GNDs densities of the as-fabricated sample and T6 sample are  $ 2.13 \times 10^{13}/m^{2} $ and  $ 1.72 \times 10^{13}/m^{2} $, respectively. Although the T6 treatment lowered the dislocation density, the T6 sample still contains a high density of dislocations.

The T6 heat treatment is proved to homogenize the element distribution in the LPBF 2195 sample, further analyses are necessary to study the precipitate transformation. Fig. 14(a)(b) presents the high-angle annular dark field (HAADF) micrographs of the as-fabricated LPBF 2195 sample. Three kinds of precipitates with different contrast (white, grey, and black), which are hardly distinguishable in SEM and EPMA, are found in the HAADF results. Combining with the EDS results of Al, Cu, Mg, and O in Fig. 14(c), the white precipitates (white arrows) are rich in both Cu and Mg. These white precipitates are either round or elongated. The grey precipitates (grey arrows) that are rich in O can be  $ Al_{2}O_{3} $. The oxidation can originate from either the oxide layer on the powder surface or the oxygen in the building chamber. None of Cu, Mg, and O is rich in the black precipitates, and the aluminum content of the black precipitates (black arrows) is significantly lower than the Al matrix, indicating another element is rich in this phase. As various alloying elements are added to the 2195 alloy, the element distribution and precipitate type are very complex in the as-fabricated LPBF 2195 sample. Fig. 15 presents the bright field micrographs of the T6 LPBF 2195 sample. Many T₁ plates precipitate in the area with the high density of dislocation as displayed in Fig. 15(a). Although the T₁ phase preferentially nucleates and grows on dislocations, there are also T₁ plates at the location with the low density of dislocation as shown in Fig. 15(b). Dislocation movement can be effectively impeded by these T₁ plates.

### 3.4. Tensile properties

As shown in Fig. 16, the as-fabricated LPBF 2195 ample has the mechanical property of the yield stress (YS) of  $ 207.7 \pm 1.5 $ MPa, the UTS of  $ 340.7 \pm 2.9 $ MPa, and the elongation of  $ 17.2 \pm 0.9\% $. After T6 treatment, the YS and the UTS are significantly improved to  $ 521.3 \pm 14.4 $ MPa and  $ 587.0 \pm 7.2 $ MPa, and the elongation is  $ 6.9 \pm 0.3\% $. The  $ T_{1} $ precipitates offer a better hardening effect than any other precipitates in 2195 alloy because of the high aspect ratio of the  $ T_{1} $ plates [32]. The strengthening  $ T_{1} $ precipitates significantly enhance the tensile strength of the T6 LPBF 2195 alloy.

Table 3 lists the tensile properties of the T6 LPBF 2195 samples, and the commercial plate samples under T6 and T8 conditions. The tensile properties of the T6 LPBF 2195 sample are better than the commercial AA2195-T6 plate and similar to the commercial AA2195-T8 plate with work hardening [36]. The AA2195-T6 plate requires up to 180 h of the aging duration, and the AA2195-T8 plate requires not only 20 h of the aging duration but also 2.5% of the deformation before aging. For the LPBF 2195 samples, the aging response is much faster (only 6 h). As confirmed from the KAM results, a high density of dislocations generated during the process due to the rapid cooling rate of the LPBF. And the T6 treatment did not decrease the dislocation density too much. These dislocations can accelerate the precipitation of the T₁ phase, thus the LPBF 2195 sample respond faster.

The fracture surfaces of the as-fabricated (Fig. 17(a)) and T6 (Fig. 17(b)) LPBF 2195 samples were observed at low and high magnification. Columnar grain features are observed for both types of samples, indicating that some cracks initiated at and propagated along the columnar grain boundaries during the fracture. The fine dimples that are found in the insets indicate the plasticity of the as-fabricated samples and T6 samples. After T6 treatment, many T₁ plates precipitate in the Al matrix according to the TEM results. These T₁ plates help to pin the dislocations inside the grains, which makes the cracks easier to initiate at the columnar grain boundaries. So the columnarity is more pronounced for the T6 treat sample.

### 4. Conclusions

We have proved the feasibility of the LPBF of the 2195 Al–Li alloy by fabricating nearly full dense samples without cracks after optimizing the processing parameters. The crack formation and inhibition mechanisms were investigated. The microstructure and mechanical properties of the LPBF 2195 samples under the as-fabricated and T6 treatment conditions were systematically investigated.

The optimized parameters of the LPBF 2195 samples are the laser power of 200 W, the layer thickness of 30  $ \mu $m, the scanning velocity of 80 mm/s, and the hatch spacing of 0.1 mm. The relative density of these optimized LPBF 2195 samples is 99.9%. Although some Li was lost after processing, the chemical composition of the LPBF 2195 sample still meets the standard of AA2195.

The crack formation mechanism for the LPBF 2195 alloy is that the liquid phase cannot completely feed the long channels among the columnar grains during the solidification. The high molten pool lifetime induced by the low scanning velocity can provide enough healing duration to inhibit the cracks for the LPBF 2195 samples.



The microstructure of the LPBF 2195 sample mainly consists of coarse columnar grains. The precipitate distribution and element distribution are inhomogeneous in the as-fabricated sample. After T6 treatment, the heterogeneous microstructure becomes homogeneous, the columnarity character nearly remains unchanged. The heat treatment causes the  $ T_{1} $ plates to precipitate and the high density of dislocations slightly decreased

The as-fabricated sample without heat treatment shows a YS of  $ 207.7 \pm 1.5 $ MPa, a UTS of  $ 340.7 \pm 2.9 $ MPa, and elongation of  $ 17.2 \pm 0.9\% $. After T6 treatment, the YS and the UTS are significantly improved to  $ 521.3 \pm 14.4 $ MPa and  $ 587.0 \pm 7.2 $ MPa due to the formation of the dominant strengthening  $ T_{1} $ precipitates. The mechanical properties of the T6 treated samples are similar to the commercial work-hardened AA2195-T8 plate. The aging response is much faster as the high density of dislocations accelerates the precipitation of the  $ T_{1} $ phase. Our results prove that the high strength Al–Li alloy can be applied in LPBF. Future works will focus on solving the cracking problem to elevate the building rate.

# A high strength Al–Li alloy produced by laser powder bed fusion: Densification, microstructure, and mechanical properties
Yang Qi $ ^{a} $, Hu Zhang $ ^{a,b,*} $, Xiaojia Nie $ ^{a} $, Zhiheng Hu $ ^{a} $, Haihong Zhu $ ^{a} $, Xiaoyan Zeng $ ^{a} $

For these high strength wrought aluminum alloys, hot cracks are difficult to prevent in the intolerable microstructures during the LPBF process. By optimizing the processing parameters, the UTS of the nearly full dense and crack-free Al–Cu–Mg parts is 402 MPa [16], and can be improved to 532 MPa after heat treatment [17]. The 7xxx series aluminum alloys are difficult to fabricate by LPBF in a wide range of processing parameters without cracks [18,19]. Adding grain refiners has been demonstrated extensively as an effective method of producing crack-free high strength aluminum alloy [20–26]. These grain refiners can promote heterogeneous nucleation sites and induce columnar to equiaxed transition to reduce crack susceptibility. However, the strength of most modified high strength aluminum alloys is lower than 500 MPa [20,26–28]. One exception is an Al-4.5Mn-1.3Mg-0.8Sc-0.7 Zr alloy. This alloy was developed with good LPBF processability [29], and a UTS of 570 MPa was achieved [30]. But the alloying elements of Mn, Sc, and Zr increase the density of aluminum alloy. For the eternal pursuit of high performance and lightweight, there is still a strong motivation to seek stronger and lighter aluminum alloys for LPBF.
Al–Li alloys offer higher performance and lighter weight than conventional high strength aluminum alloy in traditional manufacturing [31,32]. However, there are few reports on the LPBF of Al–Li alloys. Al–Li alloys are susceptible to hot cracks during laser processing due to their large solidification range, high thermal expansion coefficient, and the low melting point eutectic formation during solidification [33]. Recently, Raffias [34] produced a crack-free dense AA2099 Al–Cu–Li alloy LPBF part, but the highest microhardness was only 72.8 HV and no tensile properties were reported.
In this study, we chose the 2195 alloy as a demonstration Al–Li alloy for LPBF. The 2195 Al–Cu–Li–Mg–Ag–Zr alloy is one of the representative latest generations of Al–Li alloys and has wide application prospects in the military and aerospace industries [35–38]. We studied the feasibility of the 2195 alloy produced by LPBF. The processing parameters were optimized to obtain crack-free and nearly full dense

### 3. Results and discussions
#### 3.1. Densification and defects
Fig. 2 shows the optical microscope graphs of the LPBF 2195 samples produced with different scanning velocity and layer thickness. The laser power and the hatch spacing were kept 200 W and 0.1 mm. The corresponding relative densities are given in Fig. 3. Two main defects, unfused pores and cracks, are found in the OM graphs. For the samples manufactured with a fixed layer thickness of 40  $ \mu $m shown in Fig. 2(a)–(d), the relative density decreases rapidly with the increase of the scanning velocity. The decrease of the relative density is related to the increase of the unfused pores. These unfused pores are caused by the poor bonding among the scanning tracks and building layers because of the insufficient laser energy input. As shown in Fig. 2(e)–(h) and Fig. 3, the porosity of the samples fabricated with the layer thickness of 30  $ \mu $m is significantly reduced. The positive correlation between the relative density and the scanning velocity is consistent with the samples built with the layer thickness of 40  $ \mu $m. When the scanning velocity is larger than or equal to 160 mm/s, the cracks are observed for the LPBF 2195 samples regardless of the layer thickness.

The MP irregularity shown in Fig. 9(a) may be responsible for the remaining unfused defects. Combining with the EBSD result presented in Fig. 5(a), the SEM image also proves the coarse columnar grains in the microstructure. These coarse columnar grains are formed by epitaxial growth through multiple building layers. Unlike other Al-Cu alloy samples fabricated by LPBF [16,43,44], the columnar grains of the LPBF 2195 sample are wider and longer. The low thermal conductivity of 2195 leads to the low solidification rate, resulting in the wider and longer columnarity. Moreover, the content of the grain refinement element Zr in the 2195 alloy is smaller than 0.16%. The low Zr content cannot provide enough Al₃Zr nuclei for the heterogeneous nucleation sites of Al [45]. As such, lacking nucleation sites also causes the formation of large columnar grains.

As various alloying elements are added to the 2195 alloy, the element distribution and precipitate type are very complex in the as-fabricated LPBF 2195 sample. Fig. 15 presents the bright field micrographs of the T6 LPBF 2195 sample. Many T₁ plates precipitate in the area with the high density of dislocation as displayed in Fig. 15(a). Although the T₁ phase preferentially nucleates and grows on dislocations, there are also T₁ plates at the location with the low density of dislocation as shown in Fig. 15(b). Dislocation movement can be effectively impeded by these T₁ plates.
### 3.4. Tensile properties
As shown in Fig. 16, the as-fabricated LPBF 2195 ample has the mechanical property of the yield stress (YS) of  $ 207.7 \pm 1.5 $ MPa, the UTS of  $ 340.7 \pm 2.9 $ MPa, and the elongation of  $ 17.2 \pm 0.9\% $. After T6 treatment, the YS and the UTS are significantly improved to  $ 521.3 \pm 14.4 $ MPa and  $ 587.0 \pm 7.2 $ MPa, and the elongation is  $ 6.9 \pm 0.3\% $. The  $ T_{1} $ precipitates offer a better hardening effect than any other precipitates in 2195 alloy because of the high aspect ratio of the  $ T_{1} $ plates [32]. The strengthening  $ T_{1} $ precipitates significantly enhance the tensile strength of the T6 LPBF 2195 alloy.
Table 3 lists the tensile properties of the T6 LPBF 2195 samples, and the commercial plate samples under T6 and T8 conditions. The tensile properties of the T6 LPBF 2195 sample are better than the commercial AA2195-T6 plate and similar to the commercial AA2195-T8 plate with work hardening [36]. The AA2195-T6 plate requires up to 180 h of the aging duration, and the AA2195-T8 plate requires not only 20 h of the aging duration but also 2.5% of the deformation before aging. For the LPBF 2195 samples, the aging response is much faster (only 6 h). As confirmed from the KAM results, a high density of dislocations generated during the process due to the rapid cooling rate of the LPBF. And the T6 treatment did not decrease the dislocation density too much. These dislocations can accelerate the precipitation of the T₁ phase, thus the LPBF 2195 sample respond faster.

A high strength Al–Li alloy produced by laser powder bed fusion: Densification, microstructure, and mechanical properties
Yang Qi $ ^{a} $, Hu Zhang $ ^{a,b,*} $, Xiaojia Nie $ ^{a} $, Zhiheng Hu $ ^{a} $, Haihong Zhu $ ^{a} $, Xiaoyan Zeng $ ^{a} $

ARTICLE INFO

Keywords:
Laser powder bed fusion

Microstructure
Mechanical properties
High strength
A B S T R A C T

High strength 2195 Al–Li alloy samples produced by laser powder bed fusion (LPBF) were obtained in this study. The microstructure and mechanical properties were investigated for both the as-fabricated and T6 heat-treated samples. The as-fabricated sample showed a coarse columnar grain microstructure, heterogeneous element distribution, high density of dislocations, and complex precipitation types. After T6 heat treatment, the dominate strengthening T $ _{1} $ (Al $ _{2} $CuLi) plates precipitated in the matrix, and the dislocation density remained high. The as-fabricated 2195 sample showed a yield strength (YS) of 207.7 ± 1.5 MPa, an ultimate tensile strength (UTS) of 340.7 ± 2.9 MPa, and elongation of 17.2 ± 0.9%. After T6 heat treatment, the YS and UTS were dramatically increased to 521.3 ± 14.4 MPa and 587.0 ± 7.2 MPa, and the elongation was 6.9 ± 0.3%. The LPBF of the high strength Al–Li alloy was successfully confirmed.
1. Introduction

Laser powder bed fusion (LPBF) of aluminum alloys is receiving great attention from researchers and industrialists [1–3]. Most printable aluminum alloys are Al-Si based casting aluminum alloys, such as Al-Si10Mg [4,5], Al-12Si [6,7], and Al-7Si-0.6 Mg [8]. The as-fabricated samples of these alloys feature moderate strength and ductility [9,10]. To further improve the mechanical properties, many researchers have studied the heat treatment effects on AlSi10Mg produced by LPBF [11]. Although the ductility can be significantly improved [12], the highest ultimate tensile strength (UTS) is smaller than 500 MPa [13,14]. Decorating AlSi10Mg with nano-TiB $ _{2} $ particles can improve the UTS of AlSi10Mg produced by LPBF to 530 MPa [15]. However, it only reaches the typical performance of the 2xxx and 7xxx high strength wrought aluminum alloys.

For these high strength wrought aluminum alloys, hot cracks are difficult to prevent in the intolerable microstructures during the LPBF process. By optimizing the processing parameters, the UTS of the nearly full dense and crack-free Al–Cu–Mg parts is 402 MPa [16], and can be improved to 532 MPa after heat treatment [17]. The 7xxx series aluminum alloys are difficult to fabricate by LPBF in a wide range of processing parameters without cracks [18,19]. Adding grain refiners has been demonstrated extensively as an effective method of producing crack-free high strength aluminum alloy [20–26]. These grain refiners can promote heterogeneous nucleation sites and induce columnar to equiaxed transition to reduce crack susceptibility. However, the strength of most modified high strength aluminum alloys is lower than 500 MPa [20,26–28]. One exception is an Al-4.5Mn-1.3Mg-0.8Sc-0.7 Zr alloy. This alloy was developed with good LPBF processability [29], and a UTS of 570 MPa was achieved [30]. But the alloying elements of Mn, Sc, and Zr increase the density of aluminum alloy. For the eternal pursuit of high performance and lightweight, there is still a strong motivation to seek stronger and lighter aluminum alloys for LPBF.

Al–Li alloys offer higher performance and lighter weight than conventional high strength aluminum alloy in traditional manufacturing [31,32]. However, there are few reports on the LPBF of Al–Li alloys. Al–Li alloys are susceptible to hot cracks during laser processing due to their large solidification range, high thermal expansion coefficient, and the low melting point eutectic formation during solidification [33]. Recently, Raffias [34] produced a crack-free dense AA2099 Al–Cu–Li alloy LPBF part, but the highest microhardness was only 72.8 HV and no tensile properties were reported.

In this study, we chose the 2195 alloy as a demonstration Al–Li alloy for LPBF. The 2195 Al–Cu–Li–Mg–Ag–Zr alloy is one of the representative latest generations of Al–Li alloys and has wide application prospects in the military and aerospace industries [35–38]. We studied the feasibility of the 2195 alloy produced by LPBF. The processing parameters were optimized to obtain crack-free and nearly full dense

2. Materials and methods
2.1. Materials

Gas-atomized Al–Cu–Li–Mg–Ag–Zr powders supplied by Intelligence Manufacturing Technology were the starting material in this study. Fig. 1 displays the powder morphology with the particle size distribution. The raw powder particles exhibit nearly spherical shape and have a mean particle size of 35.3  $ \mu $m. The inductively coupled plasma-optical emission spectrometry (ICP-OES) was used to analyze the chemical composition of the powder. Table 1 lists the chemical composition of the powder and the standard chemical composition range of AA2195.
2.2. Sample preparations

The additive manufacturing of the powders was performed on a self-developed LPBF machine at room temperature under a protective argon atmosphere. The specifications of the LPBF machine were given elsewhere [39]. A series of experiments by building 8 mm (length) × 8 mm (width) × 4 mm (height) cubic samples were performed for optimizing the processing parameters. The processing parameters used for the optimization were: the laser power of 200 W, the hatch spacing of 0.1 mm, the powder layer thickness of 40 μm and 30 μm, the laser scanning velocity of 80 – 320 mm/s, and a hatch style rotation of 90° between consecutive layers. The chemical composition of the sample fabricated with optimized parameters was also measured by ICP-OES and was listed in Table 1. Tensile plates (30 mm (length) × 8 mm (width) × 2 mm (thickness)) were built with optimized parameters and perpendicular to the building direction. For the tensile tests, dog-bone-shaped samples with a gauge section of 12 mm (length) × 2.5 mm (width) × 1.5 mm (thickness) were machined from these tensile plates. The heat-treated samples underwent a T6 condition. They were solutionized at 520 °C for 1 h, immediately quenched with water, and subsequently

data.caption: Chemical composition (wt.%) of the powder used in this study, the LPBF sample fabricated with the optimized parameters, and AA2195.

ocr_items.text: aged at 170 °C for 6 h and cooled in the air to room temperature.
2.3. Characterizations

All the samples for characterizations were prepared with the standard metallographic procedures. The optical microscope (OM) graphs were acquired by a Nikon EPIPHOT 300 optical microscope. Eight vertical section OM graphs were processed by ImagePro software to obtain the relative densities of the cubic samples. The microstructure features and the fracture surfaces were characterized by a FEI Nova Nano SEM 450 scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and a FEI Tecnai G2 F30 transmission electron microscope (TEM) equipped with an energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS). The electron backscatter diffraction (EBSD) results were collected by a Zeiss Gemini SEM 300 using a step size of 3  $ \mu $m, and the corresponding data were processed by AZTEC software. The average grain sizes were obtained based on the large angle ( $ > 15^{\circ} $) grain boundaries. The elements distribution was conducted by an electron probe microanalyzer (EPMA, EPMA-8050G). All the microstructure analyses were performed on the vertical section of the samples. The tensile tests were performed on a Shimadzu AG-100 kN tester at room temperature with an extension rate of 1 mm/min. The tensile tests were repeated three times for each type of sample. The solidification curves and the thermal-physical properties were calculated by the thermodynamic calculation software JMatPro using the Scheil-Gulliver model.
3. Results and discussions
3.1. Densification and defects

Fig. 2 shows the optical microscope graphs of the LPBF 2195 samples produced with different scanning velocity and layer thickness. The laser power and the hatch spacing were kept 200 W and 0.1 mm. The corresponding relative densities are given in Fig. 3. Two main defects, unfused pores and cracks, are found in the OM graphs. For the samples manufactured with a fixed layer thickness of 40  $ \mu $m shown in Fig. 2(a)–(d), the relative density decreases rapidly with the increase of the scanning velocity. The decrease of the relative density is related to the increase of the unfused pores. These unfused pores are caused by the poor bonding among the scanning tracks and building layers because of the insufficient laser energy input. As shown in Fig. 2(e)–(h) and Fig. 3, the porosity of the samples fabricated with the layer thickness of 30  $ \mu $m is significantly reduced. The positive correlation between the relative density and the scanning velocity is consistent with the samples built with the layer thickness of 40  $ \mu $m. When the scanning velocity is larger than or equal to 160 mm/s, the cracks are observed for the LPBF 2195 samples regardless of the layer thickness.

3.2. Crack formation and inhibition mechanism
To investigate the crack initiation and propagation, EBSD characterizations were performed for the LPBF 2195 samples with and

data.caption: Relationship between the scanning velocity and the molten pool lifetime on the centerline of the molten pool top.

ocr_items.text: without cracks (Fig. 4). Regardless of cracks, the microstructures of all the samples mainly consist of large columnar grains. The cracks initiate at and grow along the columnar grain boundaries. And some cracks originate from unfused defects due to the notch effect. During the solidification, the columnar grains are formed along the thermal gradient direction, and the interdendritic liquid remains among the columnar grains boundaries. At the final stage of the solidification, due to lack of the liquid feeding, the solidification and thermal shrinkage lead the intergranular areas to crack. The EBSD results present that the average grain sizes of the LPBF 2195 samples fabricated with the scanning velocities of 80 mm/s, 160 mm/s, and 240 mm/s are 37.9  $ \mu $m, 33.2  $ \mu $m, and 29.8  $ \mu $m, respectively. A higher scanning velocity leads to smaller average grain size for the LPBF 2195 samples. The hot crack mechanism of the LPBF high-entropy alloy is that the formation of large grains causes severe residual stress [40]. However, the hot crack sensitivity of the LPBF 2195 samples is higher for the smaller grains.

The hot crack sensitivity of alloys can be determined by their solidification curves. The solidification curves of the 2195 and the AlSi10Mg alloys are drawn in Fig. 5. The shape of these curves is determined by the alloy composition. The solidification range of 2195 (144 °C) is much larger than that of AlSi10Mg (36 °C). It is worth noting that the solidification curve of 2195 shows a sharp decline at the high solid fraction due to the increase in solute content in the liquid phase. But the solidification curve of AlSi10Mg decreases rapidly at the low solid fraction.

Based on the solidification curves, we can draw a schematic diagram of the liquid feeding between two columnar grains for 2195 and AlSi10Mg, as shown in Fig. 6. The solidification channel of 2195 is significantly longer than that of AlSi10Mg. This longer solidification channel needs more liquid phases to prevent cracking.

The liquid phase amount is positively correlated to the molten pool (MP) lifetime. The MP lifetime on the centerline of the MP top is equal to the MP length divided by the scanning velocity. Fig. 7 displays the schematic diagram of the MP length. The MP length is the distance between the two points of the solidus isotherm on the x-axis.

3.3. Microstructure
The microstructures of the crack-free LPBF 2195 samples fabricated with the optimized parameters were characterized under the conditions

MP boundaries and columnar grains boundaries are clearly distinguishable from the MP interiors. The MP irregularity shown in Fig. 9(a) may be responsible for the remaining unfused defects. Combining with the EBSD result presented in Fig. 5(a), the SEM image also proves the coarse columnar grains in the microstructure. These coarse columnar grains are formed by epitaxial growth through multiple building layers. Unlike other Al-Cu alloy samples fabricated by LPBF [16,43,44], the columnar grains of the LPBF 2195 sample are wider and longer. The low thermal conductivity of 2195 leads to the low solidification rate, resulting in the wider and longer columnarity. Moreover, the content of the grain refinement element Zr in the 2195 alloy is smaller than 0.16%. The low Zr content cannot provide enough Al₃Zr nuclei for the heterogeneous nucleation sites of Al [45]. As such, lacking nucleation sites also causes the formation of large columnar grains.
There are three different forms of the precipitates (white phases) in the SEM image with higher magnification in Fig. 9(b). Some

At the final stage of the columnar grain solidification, the eutectic phases with low melting point precipitate along the columnar grain boundaries. The precipitates inside the MP are nearly granular and evenly distributed. The fast cooling rate makes these granular precipitates uniformly distributed inside the individual MP. This heterogeneous microstructure of the as-fabricated sample is further verified in the EPMA image shown in Fig. 10(a). The Al, Cu, Mg, and Ag elements are inhomogeneous in the sample. Specifically, the counts of the Cu and Ag elements at the columnar grain and MP boundaries are higher than other locations, and the Al and Mg elements display opposite characteristics. No significant Zr segregation is observed. After T6 treatment, the T6 LPBF 2195 sample displays a uniform microstructure. The Mg and Ag elements are homogeneous, and some Cu-containing particles are evenly distributed. The T6 treatment makes these alloying elements dissolved into the Al matrix and uniformly precipitate.

Fig. 11 displays the EBSD inverse pole figure maps of the as-fabricated and T6 LPBF 2195 samples. The T6 LPBF 2195 sample also performs the coarse columnarity features. Fig. 12(a)(b) presents the grain size distribution detected by the EBSD results. The average grain sizes of the as-fabricated and T6 LPBF 2195 samples are 37.9  $ \mu $m and 34.3  $ \mu $m, respectively. These similar average grain sizes indicate that the T6 treatment did not coarsen the grains of the LPBF 2195 samples. The misorientation angle distribution presented in Fig. 12(c)(d) shows that the frequencies of the misorientation angle in the range of 0~5° of the as-fabricated and T6 samples are 13.0% and 11.2%, respectively. These high frequencies indicate that both the samples perform the preference of the low angle grain boundaries (LAGBs). These LAGBs consist of a high density of dislocations to accommodate large residual thermal strains due to the rapid cooling of LPBF.

Based on the LAGBs (0~5°), the EBSD kernel average misorientation (KAM) maps of the as-fabricated and T6 LPBF 2195 samples are shown in Fig. 13. The average misorientation angles around the measured points are given by KAM and can be used to calculate the Geometrically Necessary Dislocations (GNDs) density  $ \rho_{GND} $ [47]:

where  $ \theta $ is the average misorientation angle, u is the unit length (equivalent to the step size), and b is the Burgers vector (0.286 nm for aluminum [44]).  $ u = 3\mu m $ for our EBSD measurements. The calculated GNDs densities of the as-fabricated sample and T6 sample are  $ 2.13 \times 10^{13}/m^{2} $ and  $ 1.72 \times 10^{13}/m^{2} $, respectively. Although the T6 treatment lowered the dislocation density, the T6 sample still contains a high density of dislocations.

The T6 heat treatment is proved to homogenize the element distribution in the LPBF 2195 sample, further analyses are necessary to study the precipitate transformation. Fig. 14(a)(b) presents the high-angle annular dark field (HAADF) micrographs of the as-fabricated LPBF 2195 sample. Three kinds of precipitates with different contrast (white, grey, and black), which are hardly distinguishable in SEM and EPMA, are found in the HAADF results. Combining with the EDS results of Al, Cu, Mg, and O in Fig. 14(c), the white precipitates (white arrows) are rich in both Cu and Mg. These white precipitates are either round or elongated. The grey precipitates (grey arrows) that are rich in O can be  $ Al_{2}O_{3} $. The oxidation can originate from either the oxide layer on the powder surface or the oxygen in the building chamber. None of Cu, Mg, and O is rich in the black precipitates, and the aluminum content of the black precipitates (black arrows) is significantly lower than the Al matrix, indicating another element is rich in this phase. As various alloying elements are added to the 2195 alloy, the element distribution and precipitate type are very complex in the as-fabricated LPBF 2195 sample. Fig. 15 presents the bright field micrographs of the T6 LPBF 2195 sample. Many T₁ plates precipitate in the area with the high density of dislocation as displayed in Fig. 15(a). Although the T₁ phase preferentially nucleates and grows on dislocations, there are also T₁ plates at the location with the low density of dislocation as shown in Fig. 15(b). Dislocation movement can be effectively impeded by these T₁ plates.
3.4. Tensile properties

As shown in Fig. 16, the as-fabricated LPBF 2195 ample has the mechanical property of the yield stress (YS) of  $ 207.7 \pm 1.5 $ MPa, the UTS of  $ 340.7 \pm 2.9 $ MPa, and the elongation of  $ 17.2 \pm 0.9\% $. After T6 treatment, the YS and the UTS are significantly improved to  $ 521.3 \pm 14.4 $ MPa and  $ 587.0 \pm 7.2 $ MPa, and the elongation is  $ 6.9 \pm 0.3\% $. The  $ T_{1} $ precipitates offer a better hardening effect than any other precipitates in 2195 alloy because of the high aspect ratio of the  $ T_{1} $ plates [32]. The strengthening  $ T_{1} $ precipitates significantly enhance the tensile strength of the T6 LPBF 2195 alloy.

Table 3 lists the tensile properties of the T6 LPBF 2195 samples, and the commercial plate samples under T6 and T8 conditions. The tensile properties of the T6 LPBF 2195 sample are better than the commercial AA2195-T6 plate and similar to the commercial AA2195-T8 plate with work hardening [36]. The AA2195-T6 plate requires up to 180 h of the aging duration, and the AA2195-T8 plate requires not only 20 h of the aging duration but also 2.5% of the deformation before aging. For the LPBF 2195 samples, the aging response is much faster (only 6 h). As confirmed from the KAM results, a high density of dislocations generated during the process due to the rapid cooling rate of the LPBF. And the T6 treatment did not decrease the dislocation density too much. These dislocations can accelerate the precipitation of the T₁ phase, thus the LPBF 2195 sample respond faster.

The fracture surfaces of the as-fabricated (Fig. 17(a)) and T6 (Fig. 17(b)) LPBF 2195 samples were observed at low and high magnification. Columnar grain features are observed for both types of samples, indicating that some cracks initiated at and propagated along the columnar grain boundaries during the fracture. The fine dimples that are found in the insets indicate the plasticity of the as-fabricated samples and T6 samples. After T6 treatment, many T₁ plates precipitate in the Al matrix according to the TEM results. These T₁ plates help to pin the dislocations inside the grains, which makes the cracks easier to initiate at the columnar grain boundaries. So the columnarity is more pronounced for the T6 treat sample.
4. Conclusions

We have proved the feasibility of the LPBF of the 2195 Al–Li alloy by fabricating nearly full dense samples without cracks after optimizing the processing parameters. The crack formation and inhibition mechanisms were investigated. The microstructure and mechanical properties of the LPBF 2195 samples under the as-fabricated and T6 treatment conditions were systematically investigated.

The optimized parameters of the LPBF 2195 samples are the laser power of 200 W, the layer thickness of 30  $ \mu $m, the scanning velocity of 80 mm/s, and the hatch spacing of 0.1 mm. The relative density of these optimized LPBF 2195 samples is 99.9%. Although some Li was lost after processing, the chemical composition of the LPBF 2195 sample still meets the standard of AA2195.

The crack formation mechanism for the LPBF 2195 alloy is that the liquid phase cannot completely feed the long channels among the columnar grains during the solidification. The high molten pool lifetime induced by the low scanning velocity can provide enough healing duration to inhibit the cracks for the LPBF 2195 samples.

The microstructure of the LPBF 2195 sample mainly consists of coarse columnar grains. The precipitate distribution and element distribution are inhomogeneous in the as-fabricated sample. After T6 treatment, the heterogeneous microstructure becomes homogeneous, the columnarity character nearly remains unchanged. The heat treatment causes the  $ T_{1} $ plates to precipitate and the high density of dislocations slightly decreased

The as-fabricated sample without heat treatment shows a YS of  $ 207.7 \pm 1.5 $ MPa, a UTS of  $ 340.7 \pm 2.9 $ MPa, and elongation of  $ 17.2 \pm 0.9\% $. After T6 treatment, the YS and the UTS are significantly improved to  $ 521.3 \pm 14.4 $ MPa and  $ 587.0 \pm 7.2 $ MPa due to the formation of the dominant strengthening  $ T_{1} $ precipitates. The mechanical properties of the T6 treated samples are similar to the commercial work-hardened AA2195-T8 plate. The aging response is much faster as the high density of dislocations accelerates the precipitation of the  $ T_{1} $ phase. Our results prove that the high strength Al–Li alloy can be applied in LPBF. Future works will focus on solving the cracking problem to elevate the building rate.
